# Example Scenario: Brownfield Requirement

## Input

```
Add rate limiting to the existing /shorten endpoint to prevent abuse.
```

This is an enhancement to the existing, already-implemented URL shortener
service — a brownfield change against a live codebase, not a new system.

## Task breakdown (as generated by the Copilot Console's generic analysis path)

The Copilot Console's URL-shortener-specific branch handles the mandatory
use case; a brownfield enhancement like this falls through to the generic
heuristic path, which produces:

| ID | Task | Depends on | AI assistance |
|---|---|---|---|
| T1 | Clarify requirement & define scope | — | AI flags "prevent abuse" as an ambiguous, unmeasurable phrase; engineer confirms whether the actual target is per-IP, per-alias, or per-user limiting. |
| T2 | Design data model / API contract change | T1 | AI drafts a rate-limit-state schema (e.g. a sliding window counter) from the requirement text; engineer validates it against the existing `urls` table so no migration conflict is introduced. |
| T3 | Implement core logic | T2 | AI generates a first pass (e.g. a token-bucket or fixed-window limiter as FastAPI middleware); engineer reviews for correctness against the *existing* `POST /shorten` handler in `app/routers/urls.py`, not a hypothetical one. |
| T4 | Unit & integration tests | T3 | AI drafts tests for "requests under the limit succeed, requests over the limit are rejected"; engineer adds edge cases specific to this codebase — e.g. does a `409` (duplicate alias) response still count against the rate limit budget? |
| T5 | Document & review | T3, T4 | AI drafts documentation prose describing the new limiter; engineer verifies the documented limit actually matches the shipped configuration value. |

## Why this is genuinely brownfield, not greenfield

This requirement is only meaningful in the context of the already-running
`POST /shorten` endpoint defined in `backend/app/routers/urls.py` and the
domain service in `backend/app/services/shortener.py`. A correct
implementation must:

- Not change the existing `ShortenRequest`/`ShortenResponse` contracts
  (backward compatibility for any existing client).
- Return a distinct status code (`429 Too Many Requests`) that doesn't
  collide with the existing `409`/`422`/`503` error paths already defined
  in `urls.py`.
- Be added as middleware or a dependency, not by editing the domain
  service — rate limiting is an HTTP-layer concern, not a business rule,
  which is exactly the kind of judgment call the layered architecture in
  `ARCHITECTURE.md §3` is meant to make obvious.

## AI-related risk specific to brownfield work

A generic AI code-completion tool, prompted in isolation without the
existing codebase as context, is prone to regenerating a whole new
`/shorten` handler rather than augmenting the existing one — silently
dropping the custom-alias handling, the typed exception translation, or
the response schema already in place. The engineering-ownership discipline
here is verifying the AI's suggested diff against the *actual* file, not
accepting a plausible-looking replacement wholesale.

## Output validation

Because this specific enhancement is illustrative (not implemented in this
repository — the mandatory scope is the shortener itself, not every
possible follow-on hardening task), validation here would follow the same
pattern already established for the shipped code: unit tests against the
new middleware in isolation, integration tests confirming existing
`/shorten` behavior is unchanged for requests under the limit, and a
manual `curl` burst test confirming `429` is returned once the limit is
exceeded. This gap is also listed explicitly in `README.md`'s "Known
limitations" and `validation.security_review` in the Copilot Console's
output for the mandatory use case, rather than silently left unaddressed.
